explanations.10: This captures what operating system the script is running on (like macOS, Linux, etc.) so we can give the right installation instructions later.
explanations.22: This checks if either 'bat' or 'batcat' exists—they're the same tool, but Debian/Ubuntu renamed it to 'batcat', so we need to handle both names.
explanations.31: This file path is how we detect if the system is Debian or Ubuntu—if that file exists, we know we should use apt for installing packages.
explanations.61: This creates a temporary file with a unique name using XXXXXX as a placeholder that gets replaced with random characters, so multiple runs don't overwrite each other.
explanations.67: The {2} and {1} are placeholders that fzf will fill in with the line number and file name from each search result, letting bat highlight the exact matching line.
explanations.75: This returns early (exits the function) if the user cancelled fzf with Escape, or if fzf found no matches—the two different exit codes are handled together.
explanations.79: This sed command strips out ANSI color codes (the invisible escape sequences that create colored text), then cuts out just the filename which comes before the first colon.
explanations.80: Same idea as line 79, but this time we extract the line number, which is the second field after splitting by colons.
